symptoms.description: Yellow-orange powdery spots on undersurface of leaves. Spots are circular with pale yellow halos. Leaves fall prematurely causing defoliation.
symptoms.pattern: Circular spots 1-3 cm diameter with orange-yellow powder underneath
symptoms.location: Undersurface of older leaves first, spreading to younger leaves
symptoms.color: Orange-yellow powder on underside, yellow halos on top
symptoms.progression: Starts with small yellow spots, grows larger, develops orange powder, leaf falls off

causes: Fungal spores spread by wind and rain
High humidity (above 70%) and moderate temperatures (20-25°C)
Dense canopy with poor air circulation
Monsoon season in Karnataka (June-September)
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides (Bordeaux mixture 1% spray)
Systemic fungicides like Triadimefon or Propiconazole
Remove and burn infected leaves
Improve air circulation by pruning
prevention: Plant rust-resistant varieties (Sln.9, Sln.6)
Maintain proper spacing between plants
Regular monitoring during monsoon
Balanced fertilization to strengthen plants

symptoms.description: Uniform pale yellowing of entire leaves, starting with older lower leaves. Plant growth is stunted with smaller leaves. No spots or patterns, just overall yellowing.
symptoms.pattern: Uniform yellowing across entire leaf, no spots or margins
symptoms.location: Lower and older leaves first, progressing upward to younger growth
symptoms.progression: Gradual yellowing over 2-3 weeks, leaves remain attached but pale

causes: Insufficient nitrogen in soil from nutrient depletion
Leaching of nitrogen due to heavy monsoon rains
Poor soil organic matter content
Sandy soils with low nutrient retention
treatment: Apply nitrogen fertilizers: Urea (50-100g per plant)
Foliar spray with 0.5% urea solution (5g per liter water)
Add organic compost or vermicompost around base
Split application: apply small amounts monthly
prevention: Regular soil testing every 6 months
Balanced NPK fertilization (10:26:26 before monsoon)
Mulching with organic matter to reduce leaching
Cover crops or green manure during off-season

symptoms.description: Yellowing between leaf veins while veins stay dark green. Creates a distinctive 'Christmas tree' pattern. Affects older leaves first.
symptoms.pattern: Interveinal chlorosis - yellow areas between veins, veins remain green
symptoms.location: Older mature leaves near the bottom, margins curl upward
symptoms.color: Yellow between veins, dark green veins create striped pattern
symptoms.progression: Starts with slight yellowing between veins, intensifies over weeks, margins may turn brown

causes: Low magnesium in acidic Karnataka soils
Imbalance from excessive potassium or calcium application
Leaching in sandy or laterite soils
High rainfall washing away magnesium
treatment: Apply Magnesium sulfate (Epsom salt): 25-50g per plant
Foliar spray: 2% MgSO4 solution (20g per liter)
Apply dolomite lime (if soil is acidic): 200-300g per plant
Repeat every 2-3 weeks until recovery
prevention: Include magnesium in fertilizer schedule
Maintain soil pH between 6.0-6.5
Avoid excessive potassium fertilization
Regular leaf tissue analysis

symptoms.description: Small round holes in coffee berries. Female beetle bores into berry and lays eggs inside. Berries shrivel and fall prematurely. Affects quality and yield.
symptoms.pattern: Single tiny hole (1-2mm) at the berry tip where it attaches
symptoms.location: Green or ripe berries, hole at crown end (berry top)
symptoms.color: Brown/black hole entrance, berry may discolor around hole
symptoms.progression: Hole appears, larvae feed inside, berry shrivels, falls within 1-2 weeks

causes: Female beetle enters berry to lay eggs
Thrives in warm conditions (25-30°C)
Spreads from infected berries left on ground
Peak activity during berry development (Nov-Feb in Karnataka)
treatment: Spray Chlorpyriphos 20% EC (2ml per liter) during berry formation
Use pheromone traps to catch adult beetles
Strip-pick all berries (don't leave any on tree)
Collect fallen berries and destroy them
prevention: Harvest all berries cleanly, no leftovers
Remove dried berries from previous season
Install alcohol + methanol pheromone traps (10-15 per hectare)
Maintain field sanitation - no berries on ground

symptoms.description: Dark brown to black sunken spots on green berries. Spots have distinct margins and may have pink spore masses in wet conditions. Berries shrivel and mummify.
symptoms.pattern: Circular dark brown lesions with sunken centers, 5-10mm diameter
symptoms.location: Green expanding berries (6-8 weeks after flowering)
symptoms.color: Dark brown to black spots, pink fungal mass in center when humid
symptoms.progression: Small brown spot appears, enlarges, becomes sunken, berry blackens and dries up

causes: Colletotrichum fungus spreads by rain splash
High humidity and rainfall during berry development
Temperature 20-25°C ideal for fungus
Critical period: 4-10 weeks after flowering in Karnataka (Feb-April)
treatment: Apply copper fungicides (1% Bordeaux mixture) at pin-head berry stage
Use systemic fungicides: Carbendazim (1g per liter)
Spray every 2-3 weeks during berry expansion
Remove and destroy infected berries
prevention: Apply preventive copper sprays at early berry stage
Prune to improve air circulation and light penetration
Avoid overhead irrigation during flowering/berry formation
Plant resistant varieties if available

symptoms.description: Small circular brown spots with light gray or white centers and dark brown margins. Multiple spots per leaf give 'shot-hole' appearance when centers fall out.
symptoms.pattern: Circular spots 3-8mm with concentric rings: brown edge, white/gray center
symptoms.location: Both leaf surfaces, scattered across leaf blade
symptoms.color: Dark brown outer ring, pale gray or white center (bull's eye pattern)
symptoms.progression: Tiny brown dots appear, enlarge to circles with pale centers, centers may drop out leaving holes

causes: Cercospora fungal spores spread by water splash
High humidity and leaf wetness
Poor air circulation in dense canopy
Weakened plants from nutrient stress
treatment: Spray copper oxychloride (3g per liter water)
Use systemic fungicides: Propiconazole (1ml per liter)
Remove heavily infected leaves
Improve nutrition with balanced NPK
prevention: Regular pruning for air circulation
Avoid excessive shade
Proper plant spacing (2-2.5m apart)
Preventive copper sprays during monsoon

symptoms.description: Sudden wilting of entire plant despite adequate soil moisture. Leaves remain attached but droop. Roots are brown, soft, and rotting. Plant eventually dies.
symptoms.pattern: Wilting starts at top, entire plant affected uniformly
symptoms.location: Roots (underground symptoms), wilting visible above ground on entire plant
symptoms.color: Brown to black rotting roots, normal green leaves that wilt and droop
symptoms.progression: Plant wilts suddenly in 3-5 days, roots rot, plant collapses and dies

causes: Fungal infection of roots (Rhizoctonia, Fusarium, Pythium)
Waterlogged soil with poor drainage
Overwatering or heavy rainfall without drainage
Soil-borne fungi in poorly drained areas
treatment: Stop watering, improve drainage immediately
Drench soil with Carbendazim (2g per liter) around root zone
Apply Trichoderma bio-fungicide to soil
Remove mulch to dry out soil surface
Severely affected plants: remove and destroy
prevention: Ensure proper drainage in planting beds
Avoid overwatering, especially during monsoon
Raise beds or plant on slopes in high-rainfall areas
Apply Trichoderma to soil before planting

symptoms.description: Young leaves turn pale yellow while veins remain green. Severe cases lead to whitish leaves. Older leaves stay normal green. Growth is stunted.
symptoms.pattern: Interveinal chlorosis on young leaves, veins stay green
symptoms.location: Youngest leaves at shoot tips, while older leaves remain green
symptoms.color: Pale yellow to whitish between veins, veins remain bright green
symptoms.progression: Young leaves emerge pale yellow, severe cases turn almost white, growth slows

causes: Alkaline soil pH (above 7.0) locks up iron
High calcium or limestone in Karnataka laterite soils
Poor iron uptake even if iron present in soil
Overwatering can worsen iron deficiency
treatment: Apply iron chelate (FeSO4 or FeEDDHA) to soil: 10-20g per plant
Foliar spray: 0.5% ferrous sulfate solution (5g per liter)
Lower soil pH by applying sulfur: 50-100g per plant
Improve drainage if waterlogging is present
prevention: Maintain soil pH 5.5-6.5 (acidic)
Apply sulfur annually to prevent pH rise
Avoid excessive lime or calcium amendments
Regular soil pH testing

symptoms.description: Sudden wilting and drying of branches or entire plant. Small holes in stem with sawdust-like frass. Bark may peel off showing tunnels underneath. Young plants most affected.
symptoms.pattern: Entry holes (2-3mm) in main stem or branches with sawdust around them
symptoms.location: Main stem (20-50cm from ground level) or primary branches
symptoms.color: Brown entry holes, white sawdust (frass) outside, tunnels visible under bark
symptoms.progression: Holes appear, branches wilt above damage point, bark cracks, plant may die

causes: Beetle larvae bore into stem and feed inside
Adult beetles lay eggs in bark cracks
Affects weakened or stressed plants more
Active during dry season (Dec-March in Karnataka)
treatment: Insert wire into holes to kill larvae mechanically
Inject insecticide (Chlorpyriphos) into holes with cotton plug
Apply Monocrotophos paste around holes
Cut and burn severely affected branches
prevention: Regular inspection of stems, especially young plants
Apply whitewash (lime) to main stem as barrier
Maintain plant health with proper nutrition
Remove and burn infested branches immediately

symptoms.description: Progressive wilting starting from top branches, leaves dry but remain attached. Yellowing and leaf drop follows. Vascular tissue shows brown discoloration when stem is cut. Plant dies within months.
symptoms.pattern: Wilting starts at top, progresses downward, one side often worse than other
symptoms.location: Starts at shoot tips, moves down, often asymmetric (one side first)
symptoms.color: Yellow-brown wilted leaves, brown streaks inside vascular tissue when stem cut
symptoms.progression: Top leaves wilt, yellowing spreads downward over 2-3 months, plant dies

causes: Fusarium fungus blocks vascular system (water transport)
Spreads through soil, infected tools, or planting material
Enters through root wounds or root-knot nematode damage
No cure once systemic infection established
treatment: No effective chemical treatment available
Uproot and burn infected plants immediately
Do not replant coffee in same spot for 2-3 years
Disinfect tools with bleach solution after each plant
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (Sln.274, Kaveri)
Use certified disease-free planting material
Manage root-knot nematodes (soil treatment)
Quarantine new plants before introducing to field

symptoms.description: Fine yellow speckles on leaves giving bronzed appearance. Undersurface has fine webbing and tiny red/brown mites. Leaves become dull and may drop. Severe in dry weather.
symptoms.pattern: Stippled yellow dots covering leaf surface, bronze discoloration
symptoms.location: Upper and lower leaf surfaces, especially older leaves
symptoms.color: Yellow speckles, bronze or copper tint overall, mites are red-brown (visible with lens)
symptoms.progression: Starts with few speckles, spreads to cover leaf, bronze tint develops, leaf drops

causes: Mites suck sap from leaf undersurface
Dry weather and low humidity favor mites
Peak activity in dry season (Jan-April in Karnataka)
Spread by wind or contact between leaves
treatment: Spray Dicofol 18.5% EC (2.5ml per liter water)
Use Propargite 57% EC (2ml per liter)
Apply sulfur dust or wettable sulfur spray
Repeat spray after 10-15 days if infestation persists
prevention: Maintain adequate shade (reduces dry stress)
Regular sprinkler irrigation to increase humidity
Avoid excessive nitrogen (soft growth attracts mites)
Monitor regularly during dry season

symptoms.description: Large irregular brown blotches on leaves, often starting at leaf tip or margin. Spots coalesce to form large dead areas. Affected tissue becomes brittle and papery.
symptoms.pattern: Irregular brown patches 1-3cm, often V-shaped from tip
symptoms.location: Leaf tips and margins, spreads inward
symptoms.color: Brown to dark brown, dead tissue, no distinct border
symptoms.progression: Brown area starts at tip/margin, expands inward, leaf tissue dies and becomes dry

causes: Phoma fungus enters through wounds or natural openings
Associated with wet conditions and mechanical damage
Hail damage or wind-whipping predisposes leaves
Common after storms in Karnataka monsoon
treatment: Apply copper fungicide (Copper oxychloride 3g per liter)
Remove badly affected leaves
Improve plant nutrition to promote new growth
Usually self-limiting, may not need aggressive treatment
prevention: Provide windbreaks to reduce mechanical damage
Proper spacing and pruning for air circulation
Avoid working in plantations when leaves are wet
Maintain plant vigor with balanced fertilization

symptoms.description: Yellowing and browning of leaf margins (edges) while center stays green. Margins become brown and crispy (necrotic). Affects older leaves first. Berries may be smaller.
symptoms.pattern: Marginal chlorosis and necrosis - yellow/brown edges, green center
symptoms.location: Leaf margins/edges, older leaves at bottom first
symptoms.color: Yellow margins turning brown and crispy, green leaf center
symptoms.progression: Margins yellow, turn brown and dry, curl upward, entire margin becomes necrotic

causes: Low potassium in soil or imbalanced fertilization
Excessive nitrogen or calcium interferes with K uptake
Leaching in sandy Karnataka soils during heavy rains
High potassium demand during berry development
treatment: Apply potassium sulfate (50-100g per plant)
Foliar spray: 1% K2SO4 or potassium nitrate
Apply wood ash around base (natural potassium source)
Split dose application during berry formation
prevention: Balanced NPK fertilization (include potassium in schedule)
Extra potassium during berry development (Nov-Feb)
Mulching to reduce leaching
Regular soil testing for nutrient balance

symptoms.description: Small green or brown bumps attached to stems and leaf undersides. Sticky honeydew on leaves attracts black sooty mold. Leaves yellow and plant weakens. Ants often present farming scales.
symptoms.pattern: Clustered oval bumps (2-4mm) on stems and leaves
symptoms.location: Young stems, leaf petioles, undersurface of leaves
symptoms.color: Green, brown, or white waxy scales, black sooty mold on leaves below
symptoms.progression: Few scales appear, multiply rapidly, honeydew drips down, black mold grows, leaves yellow

causes: Scales suck sap from plant tissue
Spread by ants which farm them for honeydew
Shady humid conditions favor scale buildup
More common in poorly maintained plantations
treatment: Spray horticultural oil (2%) to suffocate scales
Use systemic insecticides: Dimethoate (2ml per liter)
Spray insecticidal soap on light infestations
Control ants to prevent scale farming
prevention: Prune to improve air circulation and light
Monitor regularly and treat early infestations
Encourage natural predators (lady beetles)
Ant barriers around tree base (sticky tape or grease)

symptoms.description: New leaves are small, narrow, and distorted. Internodes (stem between leaves) are shortened creating bunched appearance. Leaves may show interveinal yellowing. Growth is stunted.
symptoms.pattern: Small narrow leaves clustered together, shortened stem between leaves
symptoms.location: New growth at shoot tips, older leaves normal
symptoms.color: Pale green to yellow new leaves, may have yellow between veins
symptoms.progression: New leaves emerge small and narrow, internodes don't elongate, rosette pattern forms

causes: Low zinc in alkaline Karnataka soils
High phosphorus application interferes with zinc uptake
Low organic matter in soil
Common in newly cleared or degraded soils
treatment: Apply zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) to soil: 10-20g per plant
Foliar spray: 0.5% zinc sulfate solution (5g per liter)
Add zinc to regular fertilizer schedule
Response visible in new growth within 2-3 weeks
prevention: Include zinc in micronutrient spray (chelated zinc)
Maintain adequate organic matter in soil
Avoid excessive phosphorus fertilization
Preventive zinc spray during active growth
